description: Slow-leak abdominal aortic aneurysm with mild back pain. Stable vitals from compensation; mortality if missed.

patient.name: Frank R., 72M
patient.chief_complaint: Aching lower back and left flank discomfort for 6 hours, came on suddenly while gardening. Mild lightheadedness once when standing. Mild abdominal fullness. No injury, no trauma. Walked in slowly.
patient.history: Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, 40pk/yr smoker, known abdominal aortic aneurysm 4.5cm followed by vascular surgery (last scan 6 months ago)

rubric_notes: **CRITICAL MISS TEST.** Vitals look benign (BP normal, HR borderline tachy, SBP normal because the AAA is leaking slowly, not yet free-rupturing). NO classic 'hypotension + shock' presentation. BUT: 72-year-old smoker with KNOWN 4.5cm AAA presenting with sudden back/flank pain + lightheadedness + mild abdominal fullness = RUPTURING/LEAKING AAA until proven otherwise. The 'triad of ruptured AAA' is abdominal/back pain + hypotension + pulsatile mass, but only ~50% have hypotension on arrival. Mortality is 80%+ if not in OR within hours. Agent that misses the known-AAA + sudden-pain combination and says ESI 3-4 because vitals are stable has missed a ruptured AAA. Immediate vascular consult + bedside US + emergent CT angiogram. This is the highest-stakes critical-miss in the eval.
